answer:
**Several works in the corpus reflect on writing as an act, particularly in Old English poetry where "rune" (rune/letter/writing/mystery) evokes inscription, composition, and the scribe's craft—often with runic signatures or maxims on poetic creation amid life's transience.[^1][^2]**

### Old English Elegies (Unknown)
- **Maxims I** (Ch. 6): Explicitly lists writing runes as a virtuous act alongside speaking wisdom and singing songs: "One should speak counsel, **write runes**, sing a song, earn praise, declare glory, hasten through the day." This frames writing as essential to human endeavor and praise.[^3]
- **Ch. 15 ("Wilt thu, fus haele")**: Advises inscribing wisdom metaphorically in the mind: "the deep-thinking man, of these dark creations, **inscribe in his mind** the word-hoard's craft, make firm his spirit-mind."[^4]

### Cynewulf's Signed Poems
Cynewulf, an Anglo-Saxon poet, embeds his name in runic acrostics at each poem's end, reflecting on writing as a frail, redemptive act against mortality—begging prayers from readers who decipher ("read") his inscription.[^5]

- **Elene** (Ch. 1): Elene demands "holy runes" (inscriptions/writings) from Jewish sages; the poem closes with Cynewulf on composing: "Thus I... have **woven word-craft**... I did not know the truth of the Rood until a roomier thought... unveiled wisdom in my mind’s chamber."[^6]
- **Juliana** (Ch. 1): Features "halge rune" (holy runes/writings); ends with Cynewulf's plea amid runes spelling his name, linking writing to soul's journey.[^7]
- **Andreas** (Ch. 1): Repeated "rune" (secrets/writings, e.g., devils "gesaeton... sundor to rune"); reflects divine inscription of fates.[^8]

**No other works offer sustained reflection; Smṛti texts mention scribes ritually (e.g., legal documents), but without philosophical depth on the act itself.[^9] Modern Urdu poetry (Iqbal, Ghalib) uses "pen" metaphorically, but not as scribe/inscription reflection.[^10]**

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 6[/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/].
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, Ch. 1[/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-d2e607].
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 6, [p-84cf09](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/06-maxims-i/translation/#p-84cf09).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 15, [p-583181](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/15-wilt-thu-fus-haele-fremdne-monnan/translation/#p-583181).
[^5]: Cynewulf poems' runic endings: transliterations show rune sequences spelling "Cynewulf."
[^6]: *Elene*, Ch. 1, [p-f5d1f1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1); reflection [p-d2e607](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-d2e607).
[^7]: *Juliana*, Ch. 1[/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/].
[^8]: *Andreas*, Ch. 1[/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-bb7698].
[^9]: E.g., *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Ch. 1 (scribes in rites); *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Ch. 26.
[^10]: E.g., *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 9: "Your tongue is like a pen, **inscribed**..." [p-fc801d](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/09-majnoon-ne-sheher-chhora-to-sehra-bhi-chhor-de/translation/#p-fc801d).

args.query: scribe OR "hand writing" OR pen OR ink
